34: The three promises of Jeremiah 31:33-34 in their historical context: (1) the Torah internalized on hearts rather than carved on tablets solves the problem that generated the exile — Israel kept the external law while their hearts were far from YHWH; (2) the universal knowledge of YHWH solves the class-stratification of covenantal knowledge (prophets, priests, sages knew; the people often did not); (3) the permanent forgiveness ('I will remember their sin no more') solves the accumulated sin-debt that the Mosaic sacrificial system could cover but not finally remove (Hebrews 10:1-4: the law has a shadow ... sacrifices cannot make perfect those who draw near). The new covenant addresses precisely the structural deficiencies of the Mosaic covenant.
1: Chapter 31 is the theological heart of the Book of Consolation (chapters 30–33) and one of the most theologically dense chapters in the entire Hebrew Bible. At that time, declares YHWH, I will be the God of all the clans of Israel, and they shall be my people. The covenant formula (I will be their God; they shall be my people) expands here beyond the traditional scope — all the clans of Israel encompasses the reunification of both the northern and southern kingdoms. The northern tribes deported by Assyria in 722 BC had been effectively lost; this promise of restoration includes them.
2: Thus says YHWH: The people who survived the sword found grace in the wilderness; when Israel sought for rest, YHWH appeared to him from far away. The wilderness-grace motif evokes the Exodus: Israel found grace (chen) in the wilderness of Sinai after surviving Egypt's sword. The new-Exodus structure of the restoration anticipates a parallel experience: the survivors of the Babylonian exile will find the same grace in the wilderness of their journey home that their ancestors found after Egypt. YHWH appearing from far away is the divine initiative of restoration — he comes toward the exiles, not waiting for them to return on their own.
3: I have loved you with an everlasting love; therefore I have continued my faithfulness to you. Ahavat olam (everlasting love) — the divine love for Israel is not contingent on Israel's performance or limited to a period of good behavior. It precedes the covenant, persists through covenant violation, and will outlast the exile. Therefore I have continued my faithfulness (al-ken meshachtika chesed, I have drawn you with covenant-love): the everlasting love is the ground of the chesed (covenant faithfulness/steadfast love) that enables restoration. This verse is one of the most explicit statements of divine unconditional love in the Hebrew Bible.
4: Again I will build you, and you shall be built, O virgin Israel! Again you shall adorn yourself with tambourines and shall go forth in the dance of the merrymakers. The virgin Israel image (cf. Jeremiah 18:13) is restored: the nation that was violated and shamed in exile is addressed as virgin — not because the past is erased but because YHWH's restoration gives a new beginning. The tambourines and dancing recall Miriam's victory celebration at the sea (Exodus 15:20–21): the new Exodus will produce the same community celebration as the first.
5: Again you shall plant vineyards on the mountains of Samaria; the planters shall plant and shall enjoy the fruit. The mountains of Samaria — the northern kingdom's heartland — will be restored to agriculture. This is a specific promise to the northern tribes: the land from which they were deported will be theirs again. Shall enjoy the fruit: the covenant blessing of Deuteronomy 28:30 (you plant but shall not enjoy) is reversed. The curse of frustrated labor is lifted; the planter will eat the harvest.
6: For there shall be a day when watchmen will call in the hill country of Ephraim: Arise, and let us go up to Zion, to YHWH our God. Watchmen on Ephraim's hills (Shomron, the northern kingdom's territory) calling people to go up to Zion for worship — the reunification image in its simplest form. The northern kingdom that had been worshiping at Bethel and Dan under Jeroboam's golden calves (1 Kings 12:28–30) will once again go to Jerusalem to worship YHWH. The division of the kingdom and its alternative worship centers will be healed.
7: For thus says YHWH: Sing aloud with gladness for Jacob, and raise shouts for the chief of the nations; proclaim, give praise, and say, O YHWH, save your people, the remnant of Israel. The call to celebratory shout (rinnah) and save-cry (yoshia YHWH et-amkha) frames the restoration as a salvation-event requiring both joyful proclamation and urgent prayer. Chief of the nations (rosh haggoyim): Israel's covenant standing as YHWH's treasured people (Deuteronomy 7:6) is restored. The remnant (she'erit) — those who survived judgment — are the focus of YHWH's saving attention.
8: Behold, I will bring them from the north country and gather them from the farthest parts of the earth, among them the blind and the lame, the pregnant woman and she who is in labor, together; a great company, they shall return here. The ingathering from the north country (Babylon) and the far ends of the earth encompasses the full diaspora. The inclusion of the most vulnerable — blind, lame, pregnant, laboring — signals that the restoration is not reserved for the strong and capable. In contrast to ancient military deportations that typically drove populations mercilessly, YHWH's regathering includes those who would be left behind in any human operation.
9: With weeping they shall come, and with pleas for mercy I will lead them back, I will make them walk by brooks of water, in a straight path in which they shall not stumble; for I am a father to Israel, and Ephraim is my firstborn. With weeping and pleas for mercy (bivchi uvetachanun): the return is not triumphant but penitent — the exiles come back acknowledging the reason for the exile. Brooks of water, straight path: the new-Exodus through a well-watered desert contrasts with the wilderness wandering of the first Exodus (Isaiah 40:3–4; Jeremiah 43:19–20). Father to Israel, Ephraim my firstborn: the divine fatherhood established at the Exodus (Exodus 4:22 — Israel is my firstborn son) is here extended specifically to Ephraim, the northern kingdom's primary tribe. YHWH's paternal love for the lost northern tribes is as real as for Judah.
10: Hear the word of YHWH, O nations, and declare it in the coastlands far away; say, He who scattered Israel will gather him, and will keep him as a shepherd keeps his flock. The nations are summoned as witnesses and heralds: the God who scattered Israel will gather him. The shepherd-king image (cf. Jeremiah 23:3–4 — YHWH as shepherd of the restored flock) frames the ingathering as pastoral care rather than political conquest. The news of YHWH's regathering is to be proclaimed in the coastlands — the most distant territories — as a testimony to YHWH's universal sovereignty.
11: For YHWH has ransomed Jacob and has redeemed him from hands too strong for him. Ransomed (padah) and redeemed (ga'al) — two covenantal rescue terms. Padah is used of the Exodus redemption (Deuteronomy 7:8; Jeremiah 9:26; Jeremiah 13:5) and ransoming firstborn sons (Exodus 13:13). Ga'al is the kinsman-redeemer language of Ruth — the one who has the right and responsibility to restore lost family members to their inheritance. Applied to YHWH's rescue of Israel from Babylon, the two terms claim that the restoration is both an act of sovereign redemption and an act of covenantal family obligation.
12: They shall come and sing aloud on the height of Zion, and they shall be radiant over the goodness of YHWH, over the grain, the wine, and the oil, and over the young of the flock and the herd; their life shall be like a watered garden, and they shall languish no more. The covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 28:3–8 are listed: grain, wine, oil, flocks, herds — the fullness of agricultural prosperity. Radiant over the goodness of YHWH (venaharu el-tuv YHWH): the restored people shine with the reflected goodness they receive. Like a watered garden: the desert-scrub image from Jeremiah 17:6 (the person who trusts in flesh) is reversed — the covenant community becomes a lush, well-irrigated garden.
13: Then shall the young women rejoice in the dance, and the young men and the old shall be glad. I will turn their mourning into joy; I will comfort them, and give them gladness for sorrow. The complete social spectrum — young women, young men, old people — will rejoice together. The transformation of mourning into joy (vehafekhti eblam lesas) is a reversal of the covenant curse of chapter 16 (no mourning, no celebration — both silenced). The restoration of joy is not merely personal but communal, generational, and holistic. NT resonance: the divine comforting of those who mourn (Matthew 5:4; Revelation 21:4 — he will wipe away every tear).
14: I will feast the soul of the priests with abundance, and my people shall be satisfied with my goodness, declares YHWH. The priests — who receive their portion from the offerings brought to the Temple — will be abundantly provided for in the restoration. Saturate the soul of the priests (verivveti nefesh hakohanim deshen): the priestly system of covenant worship will function properly, with the Temple service restored and the priests receiving their designated portions. My people shall be satisfied with my goodness: the comprehensive satisfaction of covenant blessing — material, spiritual, and relational.
15: Thus says YHWH: A voice is heard in Ramah, lamentation and bitter weeping. Rachel is weeping for her children; she refuses to be comforted for her children, because they are no more. One of the most powerful poetic images in the prophets. Rachel, the beloved wife of Jacob who died in childbirth (Genesis 35:19), weeps for her children — the Israelite tribes descended from her sons Joseph and Benjamin — who are going into exile past Ramah, the deportation staging point north of Jerusalem (Jeremiah 40:1). The matriarch's inconsolable grief captures the depth of national loss. Matthew 2:18 applies this verse to the massacre of the Bethlehem infants — in both cases, Rachel weeps for children taken before their time.
16: Thus says YHWH: Keep your voice from weeping, and your eyes from tears, for there is a reward for your work, declares YHWH, and they shall come back from the land of the enemy. YHWH answers Rachel's weeping with comfort: keep your voice from weeping (minei qolech mibechi). The exile will end; the children will return. A reward for your work (yeish sachar liphe'ulatekh): Rachel's faithfulness — her love and grief for her children — will be rewarded with their return. The covenant parent-child relationship is not severed by exile but strained, and the strain will be resolved in restoration.
17: There is hope for your future, declares YHWH, and your children shall come back to their own country. There is hope for your future (yeish-tikvah le'acharitekh): the future hope that was stated in Jeremiah 29:11 (a future and a hope) is here directed at Rachel — the grieving matriarch represents all the bereaved parents of the exile. The children will return; the exile does not define the endpoint.
18: I have heard Ephraim grieving, You have disciplined me, and I was disciplined, like an untrained calf; bring me back that I may be restored, for you are YHWH my God. Ephraim (the northern kingdom, personified) speaks repentance. The untrained calf (egel lo-lummad) image is that of a young bovine that resists the yoke before being broken to it — exactly the stiff-necked resistance to YHWH's covenant that produced the exile. Ephraim's prayer — bring me back that I may be restored (hashiveni ve'ashuv) — is the classic return-formula of the prophets (cf. Lamentations 5:21). The acknowledgment that YHWH is God (attah YHWH Elohai) is the beginning of covenant restoration.
19: For after I had turned away, I relented, and after I was instructed, I struck my thigh; I was ashamed, and I was confounded, because I bore the disgrace of my youth. Ephraim's repentance continues: struck my thigh (sapaqti al-yarech) — a gesture of shock, shame, or remorse (cf. Ezekiel 21:12). The disgrace of my youth (cherpat ne'urai) is the apostasy of the early monarchy period — the golden calves of Jeroboam, the Baal worship that characterized the northern kingdom from its inception. Ephraim is not merely lamenting recent exile but acknowledging the deep ancestral rebellion that produced it.
20: Is Ephraim my dear son? Is he my darling child? For as often as I speak against him, I do remember him still. Therefore my heart yearns for him; I will surely have mercy on him, declares YHWH. YHWH's response to Ephraim's repentance is one of the most moving expressions of divine parental love in Scripture. Dear son, darling child (ben yaqir, yeled sha'ashu'im): YHWH speaks of Ephraim with tenderness. My heart yearns for him (hameh me'ai lo): the Hebrew literally describes YHWH's intestines/womb (me'im) churning with compassion — the same physical-emotional imagery used for a mother's compassion (1 Kings 3:26). I will surely have mercy on him: the doubled verbal form (rachem arachamennu) expresses the certainty and intensity of divine compassion. This verse is one of the most important contributions to the theology of divine pathos in the Hebrew Bible.
21: Set up road markers for yourself; make yourself guideposts; consider well the highway, the road by which you went. Return, O virgin Israel, return to these your cities. The road markers and guideposts on the highway from Babylon back to Israel: the returning exiles need navigational aids for the journey home. The practical instruction is also metaphorical: the path of the new Exodus must be consciously prepared and followed. Return, O virgin Israel: the address uses the same restored-virgin image as v4 — the exile does not permanently desecrate the national identity but creates the conditions for restored covenant relationship.
22: How long will you waver, O faithless daughter? For YHWH has created a new thing on the earth: a woman encircles a man. The enigmatic final clause — a woman encircles a man (neqevah tesovev gaver) — is one of the most debated verses in Jeremiah. Interpretations range from: (1) a role-reversal image where Israel (the woman) takes initiative in pursuing YHWH (the man); (2) a fertility restoration image (a woman will again be able to carry a man-child); (3) a new-creation inversion where familiar categories are transformed. The new thing YHWH creates is unprecedented — it falls outside existing categories. Whatever the specific referent, the verse signals that the restoration will involve a genuinely new act of divine creation, not merely a return to the status quo ante.
23: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Once more they shall use these words in the land of Judah and in its cities, when I restore their fortunes: YHWH bless you, O habitation of righteousness, O holy hill! The blessing formula that will once again be pronounced in restored Judah — YHWH bless you, O habitation of righteousness — is a covenantal greeting that presupposes the presence of righteousness in the place. Restored Jerusalem will deserve and receive the blessing vocabulary that exilic Jerusalem had forfeited. The holy hill is Zion, restored to its covenantal function as the place of divine presence and covenant administration.
24: And Judah and all its cities shall dwell there together, and the farmers and those who wander with their flocks. The restored land will support the full range of Judahite economic life: settled farmers and nomadic shepherds coexisting in the same territory. This represents a comprehensive social restoration — not just the return of urban population but the full agricultural and pastoral community that made up the covenant society. The land itself is recovered as the sustaining environment for covenant life.
25: For I will satisfy the weary soul, and every languishing soul I will replenish. The divine promise of comprehensive renewal: the weary soul (nefesh ayefah) and languishing soul (nefesh da'avah) describe the exhausted, depleted exiles — those worn out by the long journey of judgment and exile. YHWH's replenishment is personal and direct: he will fill what exile has emptied. This is covenant renewal as personal revitalization.
26: At this I awoke and looked, and my sleep was pleasant to me. A remarkable autobiographical aside: Jeremiah woke from a vision or dream in which these restoration oracles had been received. My sleep was pleasant to me: the content of the restoration vision produced rest and peace, in contrast to the anguish that pervades most of Jeremiah's prophetic experience (cf. Jeremiah 20:7–18 — the consuming fire, the mockery, the death-wish). The pleasant sleep is itself a sign: the restoration vision brings peace to the prophet who has been consumed by the burden of judgment.
27: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with the seed of man and the seed of beast. The repopulation promise: the land emptied by war, famine, pestilence, and deportation will be resown — with people and animals. The sowing-image applies the agricultural framework (cf. Jeremiah 1:10 — I pluck up and destroy; I build and plant) to the human and animal population. Just as a field must be resown after it has been cleared, the covenant land will be repopulated through YHWH's sovereign act of restoration.
28: And it shall come to pass that as I have watched over them to pluck up and break down, to overthrow, destroy, and bring harm, so I will watch over them to build and to plant, declares YHWH. The six-term schema from Jeremiah's call (Jeremiah 1:10) is here applied in its full positive and negative dimensions: the same divine vigilance (shaqad, watch over) that executed judgment will now execute restoration. YHWH did not merely permit the plucking-up and tearing-down; he actively watched over it. The same active divine attention will now be applied to the building and planting. The symmetry is exact and deliberate.
29: In those days they shall no longer say: The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge. The sour grapes proverb (cf. Ezekiel 18:2) reflects a popular complaint about intergenerational punishment: we (the current generation) are suffering for what our fathers did. The proverb expresses both a desire to evade personal accountability (it's not our fault) and a genuine theological confusion about how YHWH's judgment works across generations. The restoration era will render this proverb obsolete.
30: But everyone shall die for his own iniquity. Each man who eats sour grapes, his teeth shall be set on edge. The individual accountability principle: in the restored covenant community, each person will bear the consequences of their own sin, not their ancestors'. This does not deny the generational effects of sin (cf. Deuteronomy 5:9 — visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children) but establishes that the primary moral structure is individual accountability. The restoration community will be characterized by clearer lines between personal sin and its consequences, enabling genuine moral growth and responsibility.
31: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah. The New Covenant (berith chadashah) — the most theologically significant four words in the Book of Consolation and arguably the single most important phrase in Jeremiah. This is the only explicit use of berith chadashah (new covenant) in the entire Hebrew Bible. The New Covenant encompasses both kingdoms (Israel and Judah), signaling the comprehensive reunification that the restoration promises.
32: Not like the covenant that I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to bring them out of the land of Egypt, my covenant that they broke, though I was their husband, declares YHWH. The contrast with the Sinai covenant is explicit and structural: the New Covenant will be definitionally different from what YHWH made at Sinai. The Sinai covenant was breakable — and was broken (asher hemah heferhu). The New Covenant will not be breakable in the same way. The though I was their husband (anochi ba'alti bam) maintains the marriage-covenant imagery that runs throughout Jeremiah (cf. Jeremiah 2:2; Jeremiah 3:1–3) — YHWH was Israel's covenant-husband whom she abandoned.
33: For this is the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those days, declares YHWH: I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts. And I will be their God, and they shall be my people. The three defining features of the New Covenant: (1) internalized law — Torah written on hearts rather than stone tablets. The stone tablets of Sinai (Exodus 31:18; Jeremiah 34:1–4) were external; the New Covenant Torah is internal, operating from the inside of human consciousness rather than as an external standard to be obeyed. (2) The covenant formula — I will be their God; they shall be my people. (3) Verse 34 (already written) adds universal knowledge and full forgiveness. Hebrews 8:8–12 quotes vv31–34 as the longest OT quotation in the NT, identifying the New Covenant with the covenant inaugurated by Jesus' death (Luke 22:20; 1 Corinthians 11:25; Hebrews 9:15).
35: Thus says YHWH, who gives the sun for light by day and the fixed order of the moon and the stars for light by night, who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar — YHWH of hosts is his name. The cosmic permanence of YHWH's ordering power — sun, moon, stars, sea — is invoked as the basis for the permanence of Israel's covenant relationship. The fixed orders (chuqqot) of the celestial bodies are YHWH's own ordinances, built into the structure of creation. The argument of vv35–37 is: as long as these fixed orders remain, Israel's status before YHWH will remain.
36: If this fixed order departs from before me, declares YHWH, then shall the offspring of Israel cease from being a nation before me forever. The permanence of Israel's national identity before YHWH is as certain as the permanence of the cosmic order. This is not unconditional prosperity but unconditional covenant identity — Israel will always have standing before YHWH as his covenant people. The logic is creation-theology: the God who maintains the fixed orders of creation can be trusted to maintain his covenant commitments with equal reliability.
37: Thus says YHWH: If the heavens above can be measured, and the foundations of the earth below can be explored, then I will cast off all the offspring of Israel for all that they have done, declares YHWH. The second impossibility argument: measuring the heavens and exploring the earth's foundations are humanly impossible tasks. YHWH will cast off Israel only when these impossible tasks are accomplished — which is to say, never. The hyperbole communicates absolute certainty: YHWH's rejection of Israel is as impossible as human measurement of the infinite cosmos. Whatever Israel's sin, the covenant relationship has a permanence built into creation itself.
38: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when the city shall be rebuilt for YHWH from the Tower of Hananel to the Corner Gate. The physical rebuilding of Jerusalem is specified with actual topographic references: the Tower of Hananel (northeast of the city, mentioned in Nehemiah 3:1; Jeremiah 12:39; Zechariah 14:10) to the Corner Gate (northwest, cf. 2 Kings 14:13; 2 Chronicles 26:9). The specific geographic coordinates emphasize that this is a promise about a real city in a real place — not merely spiritual restoration but physical reconstruction.
39: And the measuring line shall go out farther, straight to the hill Gareb, and shall then turn to Goah. The measuring line extends beyond the pre-exile city limits — the restored Jerusalem will be larger than the city that was destroyed. Gareb and Goah are otherwise unknown locations, likely on the western and southern sides of the city. The expanding measurement signals a city that has outgrown its former boundaries, consistent with the multiply, not few promise of v19.
40: The whole valley of the dead bodies and the ashes, and all the fields as far as the brook Kidron, to the corner of the Horse Gate toward the east, shall be sacred to YHWH. It shall not be uprooted or overthrown anymore forever. The valley of dead bodies (ha'ashpot vehadeshen) — likely the Hinnom Valley (the location of the worst apostasy and the sign-act of chapter 19) — will be made sacred to YHWH rather than defiled by it. The Kidron Valley, the eastern boundary, and the Horse Gate on the east complete the circuit. The final word: it shall not be uprooted or overthrown anymore forever (le'olam). The permanent inviolability of the restored city reverses everything Jeremiah announced in chapters 7–20. The city that will be destroyed will be restored; the restored city will endure.
